questions.question: Define unification in the context of predicate logic. Explain the concept of the Most General Unifier (MGU) and illustrate with a detailed example showing the step-by-step unification process.
keyPoints: Unification finds a substitution that makes two literals identical by binding variables to terms
The Most General Unifier (MGU) is the unique substitution that makes literals identical without unnecessary bindings
MGU is unique up to variable renaming - no other unifier is more general
Example: Unify P(x, f(y), a) and P(g(z), f(b), a) yields {x/g(z), y/b}

questions.question: Explain the resolution principle in propositional logic. Describe how resolution works as a refutation technique and provide a complete example demonstrating the resolution inference rule.
keyPoints: Resolution is a sound and complete rule of inference for clause forms in propositional and predicate logic
Two clauses with complementary literals (one positive, one negative) can resolve to produce a new clause
The resolvent is formed by removing complementary literals and taking the disjunction of remaining literals
Example: From (P ∨ Q) and (¬P ∨ R), derive (Q ∨ R) by resolving on P

questions.question: Consider the following knowledge base: (1) All birds can fly, except penguins. (2) Tweety is a bird. (3) Tweety is a penguin. Use the resolution principle in predicate logic to prove that Tweety cannot fly. Show all steps: conversion to Prenex Normal Form, Skolemization, conversion to clause form, and the resolution refutation proof.
keyPoints: Convert to Prenex Normal Form by moving quantifiers to the front and standardizing apart variables
Apply Skolemization to remove existential quantifiers, introducing Skolem functions/constants
Convert to Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF) and then to clause form for resolution
Set up refutation by adding the negation of the goal (Tweety can fly) and apply resolution until empty clause is derived

questions.question: Discuss the various resolution strategies used in automated reasoning. Explain in detail: (a) Unit Resolution, (b) Input Resolution, (c) Linear Resolution, and (d) Set of Support Resolution. For each strategy, describe its working, advantages, limitations, and suitable applications.
keyPoints: Unit Resolution: At least one parent clause must be a unit clause (single literal); efficient but incomplete for general resolution
Input Resolution: One parent must be an input clause from the original KB; complete and efficient for Horn clauses
Linear Resolution: One parent must be the resolvent from the previous resolution step; space-efficient and complete
Set of Support Resolution: At least one parent must come from the negated goal or its descendants; reduces search space and ensures goal-directed reasoning
